entries.meaning: Fixed-size buffer on the stack — 256 bytes reserved. No bounds check built in. Write past 256 and you overwrite the return address.
Copy n bytes from src to dst. No safety check. Pass a size larger than the buffer and you've got a stack overflow.
Pointer to anything — no type enforced. You decide what it points to when you dereference it.
Dereference: read the 4 bytes at memory address addr and interpret them as an integer.
Stack Pointer register. Always points to the top of the call stack. Subtract from rsp to make space; add to shrink.
Pop return address off the stack, jump to it. If you controlled that address, you control execution.
No-operation instruction — CPU does nothing, moves to next byte. Used as a sled: slide execution toward shellcode.
Controlled read: you can read any memory address. Leak a pointer → defeat ASLR.
Controlled write: you can write any value to any address. Overwrite a function pointer → control execution.
Ask Windows for executable memory. The PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE flag means you can write shellcode there and then run it.
Windows type alias for unsigned 32-bit integer (0 to 4,294,967,295). Used everywhere in the Win32 API.
Query WMI for a list of all Windows services — same data as services.msc but scriptable.
Filter pipeline: only pass through objects where the condition is true. $_ means 'current object'.
Loop over every item in the pipeline and run the block for each one.
Windows access control list: an array of who has what permissions on a file, folder or registry key.
Regex match on the permission flags. Matches Write, FullControl, Modify — anything that lets you overwrite a file.
Defines a new type called pVirtualAlloc that is a pointer to a function with that signature. You can then call it like a normal function.
Get a handle (reference) to a DLL that's already loaded in the current process. Returns NULL if not loaded.
Look up a function's address inside a loaded DLL by its name string. Returns a void* — cast it to your function pointer type.
Windows type aliases: LPVOID = void pointer, SIZE_T = size in bytes (platform-wide), DWORD = unsigned 32-bit int.
Include the Windows SDK header — brings in all Win32 API types, macros, and function declarations in one line.
Get a handle to another running process by its PID. PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS means you're requesting full control. Requires matching privilege level.
Allocate executable memory inside ANOTHER process. The Ex suffix means it works cross-process. This is where your shellcode will live.
Write bytes from your process into another process's memory at the address you allocated. No read — this is a write. Your shellcode goes in here.
Spawn a new thread inside another process, starting execution at addr. That address is your shellcode. The target process now runs your code.

Pointer to a structure describing a CPU exception (crash). Passed to your vectored exception handler — contains the exception code, the faulting address, and the full CPU register state at the time of the fault.
Access the RAX register value captured when the exception fired. You can read OR write it — writing lets you change what value the instruction returns.
Register fn as the first handler called when ANY CPU exception occurs. 1 = call it first. Your handler runs before Windows' default handling.
A CPU register that, when set to an address, causes the CPU to fire a debug exception (#DB) when execution reaches that address. Set via SetThreadContext or kernel — no memory modification required.
The exception code fired when a hardware debug breakpoint is hit. Your VEH handler checks for this code to know it was your breakpoint, not a random crash.
Shellcode as a raw byte array. Each hex value is one CPU instruction byte. This is what the CPU actually executes — no function names, no strings.
XOR each byte with key. The ^ operator is bitwise exclusive-or: flips bits where key is 1. XOR twice with the same key restores the original. Breaks static pattern matching.
Loop from 0 to len-1. i is the index. Runs the body once per byte in the buffer — standard iteration over an array.
Cast the byte array to a function pointer, then call it. The CPU treats those bytes as instructions and executes them directly. This is how shellcode runs.
Module for packing/unpacking binary data. Lets you read raw bytes from a file and interpret them as integers, addresses, or structs.
Unpack 4 bytes from data as a little-endian unsigned 32-bit int. < means little-endian (Intel byte order). I means unsigned int. Returns a tuple — use [0] to get the value.
Read exactly n bytes from a file handle. Returns a bytes object. Call after f = open('file', 'rb') for binary mode.
Convert integer x to a hex string like '0x1a3f'. Useful for displaying addresses and offsets.
Slice: take bytes from index n up to (not including) m. data[0:2] gives the first 2 bytes — the PE MZ magic number.
Download a file from a URL and save it. Equivalent to wget. Commonly used for stage-2 payload pull.
Built-in .NET class for HTTP. More flexible than Invoke-WebRequest. Can download to memory: (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString(url)
Environment variables: current machine name and logged-in username. Fast, no WMI query needed.
List all users in the Active Directory domain. Classic post-exploitation recon — no PowerShell required.
HTTP POST request. Used in C2 exfil: POST stolen data to your listener endpoint.
Create a TCP socket. AF_INET = IPv4, SOCK_STREAM = reliable ordered connection (TCP). Returns a file descriptor.
Connect the socket to a remote address. addr contains the IP and port. If your listener is running on that address, you now have a C2 channel.
Send len bytes from buf over the socket. 0 = default flags. This is how your beacon sends results back to the operator.
Python TCP connect — same concept, fewer lines. Useful for scripting quick C2 listeners.
Receive up to 1024 bytes from the socket. Blocks until data arrives. Returns bytes — decode to string if needed.
Non-blocking mode — recv() returns immediately even if no data. Use with select() for multiplexing multiple connections.
Pack a 16-bit port number as big-endian bytes. Used when building raw IP/TCP packets manually.
Windows built-in port forwarding — redirect a port from one address to another. Living-off-the-land pivoting tool.
Check if a remote port is open. Equivalent to netcat -z. Use for enumerating open SMB/WinRM/RDP ports across subnet.
Open a TCP connection in PowerShell. Used for tunnelling or testing connectivity from a compromised host.
XOR with 0xFF flips all 8 bits — turns every byte into its bitwise complement. Simple single-byte key.
Rolling XOR with a multi-byte key. i%klen cycles through the key bytes repeatedly across the entire buffer.
Encode bytes to base64 string. Looks like random text, avoids binary bytes in transit. Not encryption — trivially decoded.
Compute MD5 hash of data as a hex string. Used for checksums, key derivation, or verifying payload integrity.
Generate n cryptographically random bytes. Use for key generation — never use random.random() for crypto.
Execute a string as PowerShell code at runtime. IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString(url) downloads and runs a script in one line — nothing written to disk.
Decode a base64 string to raw bytes in PowerShell. Chain with IEX to run encoded payloads from memory.
Built-in Windows certificate utility — also decodes base64 files. Commonly used to drop a binary from a text transfer.
Background Intelligent Transfer Service — a built-in download manager. Bypasses simple web proxies. Logs go to BITS event log, not browser history.
Register a COM object — but /i:url fetches a .sct script from a URL and executes it. No file on disk. Bypasses AppLocker (application whitelisting).
Load the AD module — available on domain-joined machines with RSAT or DCs. Needed for Get-ADUser, Get-ADGroup etc.
Pull all users from the domain with all attributes. -Filter * = no filter. -Properties * = include non-default attributes like LastLogon, ServicePrincipalName.
Built-in ADSI searcher — works WITHOUT the AD module, available on any domain-joined machine. Useful when RSAT isn't installed.
List all members of Domain Admins. Classic recon — no imports, works from any domain-joined cmd.exe.
List all Service Principal Names on the domain. SPNs mark accounts as Kerberos service accounts — targets for Kerberoasting.
Ask Android for the GPS/location service. Returns a LocationManager object you use to register for location updates.
Register a callback that fires every time the GPS position updates. 0 ms interval = as fast as possible. Your callback receives Location objects.
Extract decimal latitude and longitude from a Location object. Convert to DMS coordinates or send raw.
Java TCP socket — same concept as C but exception-safe. Use with InputStream/OutputStream for C2 communication.

App permissions are declared here. ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION, READ_CONTACTS, CAMERA. Without the declaration, Android blocks the API call. With it, Android asks the user — or auto-grants on install depending on version.
Look up domain registration data: registrant name, email, registration date, name servers. Often reveals org names and contact info.
DNS lookup — resolve a hostname to an IP address. First step in mapping a target's infrastructure.
HTTP GET request with a 5-second timeout. Use for scraping, banner grabbing, or checking if a URL is live.
HTTP response headers — often leaks server software, framework versions, CDN provider, and sometimes internal hostnames.
Parse a JSON string into a Python dictionary. Most modern APIs return JSON — use this to access individual fields.
Get all IP addresses for a hostname — returns IPv4 and IPv6. More complete than gethostbyname.
Query DNS for a specific record type. MX = mail servers (useful for email infrastructure mapping). Requires dnspython.
HTTP HEAD request — fetches only headers, no body. Fast way to check a URL and read server headers without downloading the page.
Port scan: -p- scans all 65535 ports, -sV tries to identify the service version running on each open port.
PowerShell DNS lookup for all record types. Returns A, MX, TXT, NS records — maps the target's external DNS footprint.
